explanation: §01.4.4 — grades and shop-drawing requirements are the same for all projects regardless of grade. Grade changes the product requirements, not the submittal requirements.

stem: Who is responsible for code and regulation research and compliance direction on a NAAWS project?
explanation: §01.4.7.1 — code/regulation research and compliance direction is the design professional's, not the manufacturer's or installer's.

stem: Who is responsible for coordinating the manufacturer's shop drawings with other trades and enforcing hold-to/guaranteed dimensions?
explanation: §01.4.7.2 — coordination with other trades and enforcement of hold-to dimensions is the general contractor's responsibility.

stem: Who is responsible for communicating design and field changes to all parties?
options: The design professional or the contractor, depending on the prime-contract relationship
explanation: §01.4.7.3 — communication of changes falls to the design professional or the contractor depending on the prime-contract relationship. This three-way split (01.4.7) is the highest-probability S01 trap.

stem: Which of these is a required item on the submittal cover/title sheet?
options: Unit pricing for the finishing schedule
The grade requirement and a table of contents
explanation: §01.4.10 — the cover/title sheet carries the project name and address, the firm contacts, the grade requirement, and a table of contents.

stem: How and when must a substitution or change request be submitted?
options: Bundled into the shop drawings, after fabrication begins
Itemized separately, no later than the time of the shop-drawing submittal
Only after the design professional requests it
explanation: §01.4.10.2.1 — substitutions/change requests are itemized separately and submitted as soon as possible, no later than the shop-drawing submittal.

stem: For a transparent-finish item, the listing states the lumber species and cut. For veneer, which additional attributes must it state?
options: Species, cut, leaf match/balance, panel match, and room match
explanation: §01.5 — veneer listings state species, cut, leaf match/balance, panel match, room match (plus flitch number and supplier if specified). Opaque finish needs species only.

stem: Which three panel-core compliance designations may a listing need to state?
explanation: §01.5 — NAUF (No Added Urea Formaldehyde), ULEF (Ultra Low Emitting Formaldehyde), and CARB (California Air Resources Board).

stem: A finishing listing must state which two things?
options: The number of coats and the dry time
The applicator and the warranty term
The NAAWS finish system number and the sheen
The paint brand and the color code
explanation: §01.5 — the finishing listing states the NAAWS finish system number and the sheen.

stem: Minimum drawing scales for reference plan / plan & elevation / detailed section / unique features?
explanation: §01.6.1 — reference plan 1/16"=1'-0" (1:200); plan/elevation 3/8"=1'-0" (1:25); detailed section 1-1/2"=1'-0" (1:10); unique features 3"=1'-0" (1:5).

stem: Casework shop drawings must additionally show which of the following?
options: The delivery route to the site
Shelf core, thickness, and load rating, plus attachment to walls/floors/ceilings including seismic
explanation: §01.6.7 — casework drawing additions include construction/field dimensions, joinery sections, door/drawer type-thickness-height-depth, shelf core/thickness/load rating, and attachment including seismic, with blocking dimensioned off finished floor.

stem: At what scale must the countertop section detail be drawn, and what must it show?
options: 1:5, showing overhang, edge type, splash type and height, drip groove, and end-splash return
explanation: §01.6.8 — countertop section detail at 3"=1'-0" (1:5) showing front/end overhang, edge type, splash type and height, drip groove, and end-splash return.

stem: How many hardware samples are required, and what happens to them?
options: One of each; kept permanently on site
Two of each; destroyed after testing
Three of each; retained by the design professional
One of each decorative and functional item; returned after approval
explanation: §01.7.1.1 — one of each decorative and functional hardware item, returned after approval.

stem: How many samples are required for HPL, CGS, veneer, solid surface, stone, and epoxy materials?
options: Three each, at the supplier's standard sample size
explanation: §01.7.1.2 — three samples each at the supplier's standard sample size.

stem: Minimum finish-sample size on a panel versus on lumber?
explanation: §01.7.2.1 — panel sample 12" x 12" (305 x 305 mm); lumber sample 3-1/2" x 12" (89 x 305 mm), labeled with job/date/parties and finish system.

stem: Minimum finish samples: how many for an opaque finish, and how many for a transparent finish?
options: Minimum 3 per color selection (opaque); minimum 3 per finish selection (transparent)
explanation: §01.7.2 — opaque: minimum 3 per color selection; transparent: minimum 3 per finish selection, showing the color and grain range expected.

stem: Section 02 (Fundamental Requirements) provides primary governance over which sections?
explanation: §02.1 / §02.5.1 — Section 02 governs Sections 05–14 as applicable, unless the contract documents state otherwise.

stem: How does NAAWS define VISIBLE SURFACES?
options: Any surface not concealed by a door
Surfaces seen by the naked eye in a normal viewing stance between 36" and 80" above finished floor, or seen from other levels above/below
explanation: §02.2 — visible surfaces are seen by the naked eye in a normal stance between 36" and 80" AFF, or from other levels. Note: different measurement family from S10's 24"/42"/80" casework AFF bands — don't cross-wire them.

stem: The design professional specifies something that deviates from NAAWS. What governs, and what is the consequence for dispute adjudication?
options: The contract documents govern, and the deviation cannot be adjudicated using the Standards as a basis
NAAWS always governs over the contract documents
The deviation is void and NAAWS applies
The manufacturer chooses which to follow
explanation: §02.4.1 — the contract documents take precedence, and such deviations cannot be adjudicated using the Standards as a basis.

stem: Who may certify conformance to NAAWS, and what is the status of a certificate issued by anyone else?
options: Only AWMAC, the Woodwork Institute, and their assignees; any other certificate is not recognized
Any AWMAC member company; others are valid for one year
The manufacturer itself; third-party certificates are not recognized
Any licensed architect; others are provisional
explanation: §02.4.3 — only the NAAWS Sponsor Associations (AWMAC and Woodwork Institute) and their assignees may certify; anyone else's certificate is not recognized as NAAWS-conformant.

stem: Which body's jurisdiction is Canada, which is the USA, and who covers the rest of the world?
options: AWMAC covers Canada; Woodwork Institute covers the USA; each is authorized elsewhere in the world
Both cover North America jointly; ANSI covers the rest of the world
AWMAC covers Canada; Woodwork Institute covers the USA; no coverage elsewhere
AWMAC covers the USA; Woodwork Institute covers Canada; neither covers elsewhere
explanation: §02.4.4 — AWMAC → Canada; Woodwork Institute → USA; each is authorized elsewhere in the world outside the other's home jurisdiction.

stem: Is priming the manufacturer's responsibility?
options: No — unless the material is furnished pre-finished
explanation: §02.4.6.1 — priming is not the manufacturer's responsibility unless the material is furnished pre-finished.

stem: Under factory finishing, what is required of concealed surfaces?
options: They must be primed on site
They must match the exposed finish
explanation: §02.4.6.2 — factory finishing requires concealed surfaces to be factory sealed.

stem: Which grade is the primary quality level, and can grades be mixed within one project?
options: Custom is primary; grades may never be mixed
Custom is primary; Premium may be specified project-wide or mixed within a project
Premium is primary; grades may not be mixed
Economy is primary; only Premium may be mixed in
explanation: §02.4.11 — Custom Grade is the primary quality; Premium Grade is the highest and may be specified project-wide or mixed within a single project.

stem: How are Premium Grade requirements visually flagged in the standard?
options: As separate table rows colored in gold
With an asterisk in the margin
explanation: §02.4.12 — Premium Grade requirements are highlighted in separate table rows colored gold.

stem: The aesthetic compliance test applies to which surface categories, and which is exempt?
options: Exposed, Exposed Exterior, Exposed Interior, and Semi-Exposed; Concealed is exempt
explanation: §02.5.5.1–.2 — the aesthetic test applies to Exposed, Exposed Exterior, Exposed Interior, and Semi-Exposed surfaces; Concealed (non-visible) surfaces have no aesthetic requirement.

stem: Are exterior environments covered by these standards, and what about interior non-climate-controlled environments?
options: Neither is addressed anywhere in the standard
Exterior is not covered; interior non-climate-controlled work is not covered for joint flushness/gap but must pass the tests before installation
Exterior is covered; interior non-climate-controlled is not
explanation: §02.5.2 — exterior environments are not covered. §02.5.3 — interior non-climate-controlled environments aren't covered for flushness/gap tolerances, but the work must meet the tests before installation.

stem: Which of the following applies only if specifically called out in the contract documents?
options: Special building code, ADA, seismic, flame spread, fire-retardant coatings, and rated fire doors
explanation: §02.5.4 — special building code/regulations, ADA, seismic fabrication/installation, flame spread rating, fire-retardant coatings, and rated fire door assemblies apply only if specified.

stem: Absent further specification, what are the default veneer requirements for a transparent finish on sheet products (Custom, then Premium)?
options: Plain sliced, book matched, minimum 0.020" (0.5 mm) nominal, Grade A — Premium tightens to Grade AA
Rotary cut, random matched, Grade B — Premium Grade A
Quarter sliced, slip matched, Grade AA — Premium Grade A
Plain sliced, Grade C — Premium Grade B
explanation: §02.5.6 — transparent default: plain sliced, book matched, min 0.020" nominal, Grade A (Premium AA). Opaque default is closed grain, Grade C (Premium B).

stem: What is the default lumber cut, and in which species are heartwood and sapwood permitted together by default?
options: Rift sawn; permitted in all species
Plain sawn; heartwood/sapwood permitted in Ash, Birch, Maple, Cherry, Elm, and Red Oak
Plain sawn; permitted in Walnut, Cherry, and Mahogany
Quarter sawn; permitted in Oak and Maple only
explanation: §02.5.7 — default cut is plain sawn; heartwood or sapwood is permitted in Ash, Birch, Maple, Cherry, Elm, and Red Oak unless otherwise specified.

stem: For a transparent finish, what is the project-wide color/grain rule, and how does Premium tighten it?
options: One species for the entire project (compatible color/grain); Premium requires it be 'well matched'
No requirement at Custom; Premium requires one species
One species per room; Premium one per project
Any compatible species; Premium allows two species
explanation: §02.5.9 — one species for the entire project with compatible color/grain; Premium tightens this to 'well matched.'

stem: What is the rule for defects on Exposed and Semi-Exposed surfaces, and what is always required at edges?
options: They must be defect-free (defects only per Sections 3 & 4 or if concealed); all sharp edges must be eased
Minor defects allowed if touched up; edges left square
Defect-free only on Exposed; edges beveled 45 degrees
Defects allowed at Custom; edges eased only at Premium
explanation: §02.5.8 — Exposed and Semi-Exposed surfaces must be defect-free (defects permitted only per Sections 3 & 4 or when concealed); all sharp edges must be eased.

stem: Minimum inside-corner radii for cutouts: HPL/TFL/CGS versus solid surface/epoxy/stone?
explanation: §02.5.17 — HPL/TFL/CGS minimum 1/8" (3.2 mm) radius; solid surface, epoxy, and natural/engineered stone minimum 1/4" (6.4 mm). Hard/brittle materials get the bigger radius.

stem: Minimum glass thicknesses: clear glazing / clear shelves / obscure / wire?
options: Clear glazing 1/4"; clear shelves 1/8"; obscure 1/4"; wire 1/8"
Clear glazing 1/8"; clear shelves 1/4"; obscure 1/8"; wire 1/4"
explanation: §02.5.20 — clear glazing ≥1/8" (3.2 mm); clear shelves ≥1/4" (6.4 mm); obscure 1/8"; wire 1/4".

stem: Section 02 closes with a blanket workmanship requirement. What does it state?
options: The manufacturer's judgment is final on aesthetics
Tolerances supersede workmanship in all cases
First-class workmanship compliance is always required within these standards
explanation: §02.5.23 — 'First-class workmanship compliance is always required within these standards' — a blanket floor beneath every grade-specific tolerance.

stem: The underside of a cabinet bottom sits at 30" AFF. Which surface category?
explanation: §10.2 — the 24"–42" AFF band is Semi-Exposed.

stem: The underside of a cabinet bottom sits at 48" AFF. Which surface category?
explanation: §10.2 — over 42" AFF is Exposed-Exterior.

stem: The underside of a cabinet bottom sits at 18" AFF. Which surface category?
explanation: §10.2 — under 24" AFF is Concealed.

stem: A cabinet top at 84" AFF is not visible from any level above. Which category?
explanation: §10.2 — over 80" AFF and not visible from above is Concealed.

stem: A cabinet top at 84" AFF is visible from a mezzanine above. Which category?
explanation: §10.2 — the 'visible from a level above' carve-out makes it Exposed-Exterior even above 80".

stem: An interior top member at 40" AFF is inside a cabinet with solid doors. Which category?
explanation: §10.2 — top members at/above 36" AFF seen only when the door is open are Semi-Exposed; behind glass or open casework the same member would be Exposed-Interior.

stem: The three non-visible edges of an adjustable shelf are which category?
explanation: §10.2 — the non-visible edges of an adjustable shelf are Concealed.

stem: The face of a cabinet end where two units butt together is which category?
explanation: §10.2 — a cabinet-end face hidden where two units butt is Concealed.

stem: The interior face of a cabinet door is which category?
explanation: §10.2 — the interior face of a door is Exposed-Interior.

stem: A contract says only 'laminate faced casework.' By default, what material, grade, construction, and door style do you build?
options: CGS, Custom Grade, frameless, inset doors
HPL, Premium Grade, face-frame, overlay doors
HPL, Custom Grade, frameless, overlay doors
TFL, Premium Grade, face-frame, inset doors
explanation: §10.3 — 'laminate faced' defaults to HPL, Custom Grade, frameless construction, overlay doors, non-premium color, manufacturer's brand.

stem: Wood-faced, opaque finish, nothing further specified — what substrate by default?
explanation: §10.3 — opaque wood-faced default substrate is MDF, manufacturer's choice.

stem: Wood-faced, transparent finish, nothing further specified — what veneer by default?
options: Red oak, plain sliced, book matched
Imported rotary birch, stained to match
Domestic hardwood veneer, manufacturer's choice, with no stain or shading
explanation: §10.3 — transparent wood-faced default is domestic hardwood veneer, manufacturer's choice, no stain or shading.

stem: CGS (compact laminate) with no finish specified — what sheen by default?
explanation: §10.3 — the default CGS sheen is satin.

stem: Horizontal reveal range for frameless casework versus face-frame casework?
explanation: §10.4.17 — frameless reveals 1/8"–1/4"; face-frame reveals 1/4"–1"; kept consistent project-wide.

stem: A wall is out of plumb by 5/16" over 144". Must you scribe casework to it?
options: No — beyond 1/4" (6.4 mm) in 144" the wall is not an acceptable substrate; correction is not the installer's responsibility
Yes — always scribe to the wall as found
explanation: §10.4 — walls out of plumb more than 1/4" (6.4 mm) in 144" are not an acceptable substrate; scribing is not required and correction is not the installer's responsibility.

stem: A floor is out of level by 3/8" over 144". Must you shim to it, and what is the shim limit?
options: Yes — the floor tolerance is 1/2" (12.7 mm) in 144", so 3/8" is within it; shim up to 1/2"
No — any deviation over 1/4" must be corrected first
explanation: §10.4 — the floor limit is 1/2" (12.7 mm) in 144", so 3/8" is within tolerance; shim to a maximum of 1/2", beyond which the floor is corrected first.

stem: Maximum bevel on each frameless edgeband, and the maximum total 'V'?
explanation: §10.4 — each frameless edgeband bevel ≤15°; total 'V' ≤30°.

stem: Above what base-cabinet height is a separately applied countertop NOT required?
explanation: §10.4 — separately applied countertops are required at base cabinets 48" (1220 mm) or less; above that height they are not required.

stem: A tall cabinet over what height (not abutting a wall/cabinet at both ends) requires a fixed mid-height shelf, and what seismic screw spec and spacing apply?
options: Over 72" (1829 mm); anchor strip with #10 x 2-1/2" (50.8 mm) screws at max 7" (178 mm) o.c.
explanation: §10.4 — tall cabinets over 72" (1829 mm) not abutting at both ends need a fixed mid-height shelf; seismic adds an anchor strip fastened with #10 x 2-1/2" screws at max 7" o.c.

stem: Minimum hinge opening angle and the ANSI/BHMA standard for casework hinges?
explanation: §10.5 — 110° minimum nominal opening; ANSI/BHMA 156.9, Grade 2 minimum hardware, dynamic-tested to 50,000 cycles.

stem: Drawer-slide load capacities for a box 24" wide or less: pencil / general / file?
explanation: §10.5.1.8 — box ≤24" wide: pencil 50 lb, general 75 lb, file 100 lb; roller or ball-bearing slides permitted.

stem: For a drawer box wider than 24": pencil / general / lateral file ≤30" / lateral file >30" — and what slide type is mandatory?
explanation: §10.5.1.8 — box >24" wide: pencil 50, general 75, lateral file ≤30" 150, lateral file >30" 200 lb; ball-bearing slides only.

stem: Maximum shelf load, and the minimum shelf-rest pin?
options: 200 lb (91 kg); a 5.0 mm metal pin or double 5.0 mm plastic pins (BHMA Grade 1)
explanation: §10.5 — maximum shelf load 200 lb (91 kg); minimum rest is a 5.0 mm metal pin or double 5.0 mm plastic pins (BHMA Grade 1).

stem: Maximum pocket (retractable) door width?
explanation: §10.5 — maximum pocket-door width is 23-5/8" (600 mm).

stem: Edgebanding thickness minimums: HPL / PVC-ABS-PP / veneer?
explanation: §10.5 — HPL 0.020" (±0.004); PVC/ABS/polyester/polypropylene 0.018" (±0.005); veneer 0.020" (±0.0025).

stem: Minimum core thickness for drawer sides/back/sub-front, and for the drawer bottom (and when does the bottom change)?
options: Sides/back/sub-front ≥1/2" (12.7 mm); bottom ≥1/4" (6.4 mm), rising to ≥1/2" when the box is wider than 30" (762 mm)
explanation: §10.5 — drawer sides/back/sub-front ≥1/2" (12.7 mm); bottom ≥1/4" (6.4 mm), rising to ≥1/2" when the box is wider than 30" (762 mm).

stem: Veneer grade for an Exposed-Exterior transparent surface (Custom vs Premium), and for Exposed-Interior?
options: All surfaces Grade A regardless of location
Exp-Ext: Grade B (Premium A); Exp-Int: Grade C (Premium B)
Exp-Ext: Grade AA (Premium A); Exp-Int: Grade A (Premium B)
Exp-Ext: Grade A (Premium AA); Exp-Int: Grade B (Premium A)
explanation: §10.5 — Exposed-Exterior transparent: Grade A (Premium AA); Exposed-Interior: Grade B (Premium A). Plain sliced, book match, one species project-wide (rotary at Birch).

stem: HPL/TFL color/pattern limits: how many per room, and how many per project?
explanation: §10.5 — 1 color/pattern per room; maximum 5 per project.

stem: Minimum thickness for CGS at an Exposed-Exterior surface?
explanation: §10.5 — CGS at Exposed-Exterior surfaces must be ≥3/8" (9.5 mm).

stem: Maximum setback of a fixed horizontal member, and the maximum 'V' gap (Custom vs Premium)?
options: Setback ≤5/64" (2.0 mm) uniform; V gap ≤0.015" (0.4 mm), Premium ≤0.007" (0.2 mm), no core showing
explanation: §10.6 — fixed horizontal member setback ≤5/64" (2.0 mm), uniform; 'V' gap ≤0.015" (0.4 mm), Premium ≤0.007" (0.2 mm), with no core showing.

stem: A filler is acceptable when inconspicuous at what viewing distance, and at which grade is it prohibited?
options: Inconspicuous at 24" (610 mm); prohibited at Premium Grade
explanation: §10.6 — a filler is acceptable when inconspicuous viewed at 24" (610 mm); it is prohibited at Premium Grade.

stem: Drawer squareness tolerance for width/length, and for the diagonal?
explanation: §10.6 — drawer squareness ≤1/32" (0.8 mm) on width/length; ≤1/16" (1.6 mm) on the diagonal.

stem: Which joinery methods are Performance Level 3, and which are Level 4?
options: L3: glued-in dowel (min 15/64" x 1-3/16", ≥2 per joint) or multiple dovetail. L4: lock joint, miter fold, dowel link, push pocket
L3: butt joint with screws. L4: glued dowel
L3: lock joint, miter fold. L4: dovetail
L3: dowel link, push pocket. L4: dovetail, lock joint
explanation: §10.6 — Level 3: glued-in dowel (min 15/64" x 1-3/16" / 6 x 30 mm, ≥2 per joint) or multiple dovetail. Level 4: lock joint, miter fold, dowel link, push pocket.

stem: Drawer-bottom dado: minimum distance from the bottom edge, and maximum plow depth?
options: ≥3/8" (9.5 mm) from the bottom; plow depth ≤40% of thickness
explanation: §10.6 — drawer-bottom dado ≥3/8" (9.5 mm) from the bottom; plow depth ≤40% of thickness.

stem: Minimum door-core thickness and permitted core materials — and which cores are not recommended?
options: ≥3/4", veneer core or lumber; not recommended: MDF
≥11/16" (17.5 mm), particleboard or MDF; not recommended: veneer core, OSB, lumber, combination cores
explanation: §10.6 — door core ≥11/16" (17.5 mm), particleboard or MDF; veneer core, OSB, lumber, and combination cores are not recommended.

stem: Maximum single-door size on the vertical hinge axis, and on the horizontal hinge axis?
options: Vertical hinge axis 24"W x 84"H; horizontal hinge axis 48"W x 24"H
explanation: §10.6 — maximum single door: vertical hinge axis 24"W x 84"H; horizontal hinge axis 48"W x 24"H.

stem: Minimum number of glass clips per door — and if the glass is glued instead?
explanation: §10.6 — minimum 6 glass clips per door; minimum 4 if the glass is glued.

prompt: A spec question asks about drawer-slide load ratings. Which tab do you jump to?
The spec is silent on grade. Where do you find the default grade rule (Custom is primary)?
You need the flatness / flushness / gap tolerance categories (A through R). Which tab?
note: The 18 lettered A–R test categories live here, not in S10.

prompt: A dispute over required shop-drawing scales (1:200, 1:25, 1:10, 1:5). Which tab?
You need the casework AFF surface-category thresholds (24" / 42" / 80"). Which tab?
note: §10.2 surface definitions — different bands from S02's 36"–80" visible-surface rule.

prompt: You need the aesthetic viewing-distance test (48" Custom / 24" Premium). Which tab?
A question about solid lumber species, cuts, and permitted natural characteristics. Which tab?
You need core types and veneer grades for panel/sheet products (HPL, particleboard, MDF). Which tab?
A question about finish system numbers, sheen, and film build. Which tab?
You need requirements for standing & running trim, casing, and moldings. Which tab?
A question about stair stringers, treads, risers, and handrails. Which tab?
You need requirements for wall and ceiling veneer/panel surfacing. Which tab?
A question about flush wood passage-door cores and construction. Which tab?
You need countertop material thickness, backsplash, and seam rules. Which tab?
A question about repairing or restoring existing historic woodwork. Which tab?
You need jobsite protection, humidity, delivery, and storage requirements. Which tab?
A question about jobsite installation, scribing, and fastening to the building. Which tab?
note: Installation — jobsite work (distinct from S10 factory assembly).

prompt: You need the hinge 110° opening / 50,000-cycle spec and the pocket-door width limit. Which tab?
A question about edgebanding thickness minimums (HPL / PVC / veneer). Which tab?
You need the formal definition of a term such as 'telegraphing' or 'blueprint match.' Which tab?
You need adhesive guidelines or the casework structural-integrity test methods. Which tab?
You need reference lists of manufacturers, suppliers, or associations. Which tab?
note: Resource guide — lookup only, not tested content.

prompt: A question about what the submittal cover/title sheet must contain. Which tab?
You want the guide to how the standard is organized and its legend/how-to-read notes. Which tab?
note: Front matter — how to read the standard.

prompt: TRAP: You see '36"–80" above finished floor.' Which tab confirms this is the VISIBLE-SURFACE definition (not a casework AFF band)?
note: §02.2 visible surfaces = 36"–80". S10's 24"/42"/80" AFF bands are a different family — don't cross-wire.

prompt: TRAP: A jobsite fastener schedule and scribing an out-of-level floor. S10 or S14?
note: Jobsite installation fastening/scribing is S14; factory assembly fastening is S10.

prompt: TRAP: S10 references the tolerance test categories but does not list them. Which tab actually defines the A–R categories?
note: §02.5.22 points to Section 15; the lettered A–R categories are defined in S15.

prompt: What must the manufacturer do to obtain and maintain GIS status? Which GIS section?
A dispute over the appeal / mediation procedure. Which GIS section?
GIS Inspector duties, qualifications, and conduct. Which section?
GIS project fees and financial terms. Which section?
Owner and design-professional responsibilities under GIS. Which section?
AWMAC National's role in the GIS program. Which section?
The GIS Committee's composition and authority. Which section?
Regional AWMAC Chapter responsibilities. Which section?
Consequences of non-compliance and enforcement. Which section?
Definitions of GIS-specific terms. Which section?
The overall purpose and overview of the GIS program. Which section?

stem: In the AWMAC Policy and Procedures Manual, what does the acronym "GIS" stand for?
explanation: Definitions — GIS = Guarantee and Inspection Service.

stem: What does "MSE" stand for?
explanation: Definitions / MSE Policy — MSE = Manufacturer Standards Exam.

stem: Per the Definitions, who is generally meant by the "Design Professional (DP)"?
explanation: Definitions — Design Professional (DP): generally the Architect or Interior Designer.

stem: How many regional AWMAC Chapters administer the GIS across Canada?
explanation: Definitions ("one of eight regional Chapters") and the Section 7.4 hub list, which names eight Chapters (BC, Northern Alberta, Southern Alberta, Saskatchewan, Manitoba, Ontario, Quebec, Atlantic).

stem: How is the "Effective Date of Guarantee" defined?
options: The date the contract is awarded to the Manufacturer
The date of the final site inspection, regardless of deficiencies
The date the GIS inspection report declares the project deficiency-free, OR the date the Manufacturer and Chapter declare deficiencies rectified — whichever is earliest
The date the Certificate of Guarantee is signed by the AWMAC President
explanation: Definitions — Effective Date of Guarantee: date of the GIS inspection report declaring deficiency-free status, or the date the Manufacturer and Chapter declare the deficiencies rectified, whichever date is earliest.

stem: In AWMAC documents, "AWMAC Standards" refers to which document?
options: NAAWS — the North American Architectural Woodwork Standards
The GIS Policy and Procedures Manual
The Inspector Certification Exam study guide
explanation: Definitions — AWMAC Standards = AWMAC's quality standards, NAAWS (North American Architectural Woodwork Standards).

stem: The exam a prospective GIS Inspector must pass is abbreviated ICE. What is it?
explanation: Definitions — ICE = Inspector Certification Exam (distinct from the MSE, which is for Manufacturer Members).

stem: A Certificate of Guarantee is issued only if the woodwork Manufacturer holds what status, and as of when?
options: A Certified GIS Inspector, at time of final inspection
An AWMAC Member in Good Standing, at time of tender
An AWMAC Member in Good Standing, at time of contract award
Any registered woodwork manufacturer, at time of installation
explanation: §1.4 — the Certificate of Guarantee is issued if the Manufacturer is an AWMAC Member in Good Standing at time of tender.

stem: The AWMAC Guarantee is underwritten by three parties. What is the order of default?
options: AWMAC National, then Local Chapter, then Manufacturer
Local Chapter, then Manufacturer, then AWMAC National
Manufacturer, then Local AWMAC Chapter, then AWMAC National
Manufacturer, then AWMAC National, then Local Chapter
explanation: §1.5 — in order of default: (a) Manufacturer, (b) Local AWMAC Chapter, (c) AWMAC National. The Manufacturer is the primary guarantor; AWMAC National is the third guarantor.

stem: For a Manufacturer that is NOT an AWMAC Member, what does AWMAC strongly suggest the Design Professional specify in lieu of the Certificate of Guarantee?
options: A two-year maintenance bond to the full value of the architectural woodwork contract
A one-year workmanship warranty from the installer
A cash retention held by the Owner for two years
A performance bond equal to 10% of the contract
explanation: §1.4.1 / §1.11 — non-member manufacturers provide the Owner a two-year maintenance bond, in lieu of the AWMAC Certificate of Guarantee, to the full value of the architectural woodwork contract.

stem: Under what condition is the GIS program effective, such that a Guarantee can be issued?
options: When the Manufacturer voluntarily requests an inspection
When the Owner pays the GIS fee directly to AWMAC National
When the Design Professional waives their own review
When the tender documents specify the GIS requirements and the full process is followed
explanation: §1.7 — the GIS program is only effective when the tender documents specify the GIS requirements; inspection is integral, so the Guarantee issues only if the process is followed completely.

stem: Does specifying the GIS program replace the Design Professional's own review of the woodwork?
options: Yes — the GIS inspection replaces the DP review entirely
No — it does not replace the DP's review and may occur before, during, or after it
Yes — but only on projects over $100,000
No — the DP review is only required if the Manufacturer is a non-member
explanation: §1.8 — specifying GIS relieves the Owner of the work/liability of ensuring compliance but does NOT replace the Design Professional's review; it may be done before, during, or after that review.

stem: During the final inspection, what determines whether a secondary final inspection and report is required?
options: Any deficiency at all triggers a second inspection
Only deficiencies the Owner disputes in writing
Non-compliant issue(s) considered major (not minor, e.g. a cabinet door hinge adjustment)
Only deficiencies found in the finishing section
explanation: §1.13.3.a — if there are non-compliant issues considered major (not minor, such as a cabinet door hinge adjustment), a secondary final inspection and report will be done.

stem: AWMAC and the local Chapter(s) will guarantee a project for two years to a Manufacturer Member only if all conditions are met. Which of the following is one of those conditions?
options: The Member is Manufacturer Standards Exam (MSE) compliant
The Member has completed at least ten prior GIS projects
The Member carries a separate maintenance bond in addition to the Guarantee
The Member's plant is located within the Chapter's hub area
explanation: §5.8 — conditions are: (a) the woodwork meets or exceeds AWMAC Standards, (b) the Member is in Good Standing throughout the project, and (c) the Member is MSE compliant.

stem: What are AWMAC Members prohibited from doing regarding the GIS specification, and what is the consequence?
options: Charging the Owner for the GIS; consequence is a fee refund
Recommending GIS to competitors; consequence is a caution letter
Submitting shop drawings late; consequence is loss of Good Standing
Negotiating or requesting that the GIS specification be deleted; consequence is disciplinary action under Section 9
explanation: §5.5 / §5.5.1 — AWMAC Members will not negotiate or request the GIS specification be deleted; any Member found to have done so is subject to disciplinary action (Section 9, Compliance).

stem: How must the Manufacturer include the cost of the GIS at tender?
options: As a clearly itemized separate line item so the Owner can see it
As part of the tendered lump sum, never as a separate line item
As a percentage added after contract award
Billed directly to the Owner by the Chapter, outside the tender
explanation: §5.13.2 — include the cost of the GIS as part of the tendered lump sum, never as a separate line item; use the GIS Rate Schedule.

stem: The contract uses vague wording ("Guarantee", "AWMAC Inspection") but is not a clear GIS reference. What must the Manufacturer do?
options: Omit the GIS cost since the reference is unclear
Wait for the Design Professional to clarify before tendering
Include the GIS cost at tender; the cost may be removed only if an addendum clearly states the GIS is excluded
Include the GIS cost as a separate itemized allowance
explanation: §5.13.5 / §5.13.6 — with imprecise wording the Manufacturer includes the GIS cost at tender, advises the Chapter, and requests corrected wording; the cost may be removed only if an addendum clearly excludes the GIS.

stem: After receiving the Submittal Review Report, how long does the Manufacturer have to comment before the Chapter forwards it to the DP and GC?
explanation: §5.14.4–.5 — the Manufacturer has three working days to comment; the Chapter forwards the report (with any comments) to the DP and GC after three business days. The same three-day comment window applies to mock-up and final reports.

stem: A mock-up (sample unit) inspection is mandatory if any one of several conditions is met. Which is one such condition?
options: The Design Professional has waived the submittal review
The Manufacturer has not performed a GIS project within the past two years
explanation: §5.15.5 — a mock-up is mandatory if: (a) specified by the DP; (b) the Manufacturer has not performed a GIS project within the past two years; and/or (c) has not completed a GIS project without an AWMAC Standards Tier penalty within the past two years.

stem: What temperature and humidity variance tolerance defines the acceptable range for delivery/installation conditions?
options: ± five percent humidity and ± two degrees Celsius
± three percent humidity and ± one degree Celsius
± ten percent humidity and ± three degrees Celsius
± two percent humidity and ± five degrees Celsius
explanation: §5.16.3/.4 — conditions are in variance at ± three percent (humidity) and ± one degree (Celsius), reflecting meter tolerances; meters that cannot be certified to within that tolerance are not approved for use (§5.16.5).

stem: Where are site temperature and humidity readings to be taken?
options: Immediately adjacent to the installed woodwork
At the centre of the room
explanation: §5.16.2 — temperature and humidity readings are to be taken at the centre of the room.

stem: How often must the Manufacturer's primary temperature and humidity meter be certified to NIST standards?
explanation: §5.16.5 — the Manufacturer must own at least one meter maintained and certified to NIST standards annually; additional meters must be certified/tested against a certified device semi-annually.

stem: As a guideline, a "minor deficiency" is one the Inspector believes can be addressed within what limits?
options: Four person-hours regardless of project value
One business day for any project
Sixteen person-hours for projects under $100,000, or thirty-two if over $100,000
Eight person-hours for projects under $100,000, or sixteen person-hours if over $100,000, with no major material replacement
explanation: §5.17.8.a — minor deficiency guideline: within eight person-hours for projects under $100,000, or sixteen person-hours if over $100,000, and no major material replacement required.

stem: When a project subject to a Guarantee has MAJOR deficiencies, the remedial work must be completed within what timeframe before the Guarantee Application process is initiated?
explanation: §5.17.9 — for major deficiencies the Guarantee Application process is not initiated until the remedial work is completed (within one month), inspected, and compliant. (Minor deficiencies carry a two-week guideline, §5.17.8.)

stem: If no grade is stated in the contract documents, which grade is the default?
explanation: §5.11 — if no grade is stated in the contract documents, the default is Custom Grade. (This mirrors NAAWS §02.4.11, where Custom is the primary quality level.)

stem: The GIS defines three distinct types of exclusions that may appear on a Guarantee. Which set is correct?
options: Contract-specified items in variance to AWMAC Standards; project conditions beyond the Manufacturer's control; and Manufacturer compliance issues
Aesthetic defects, structural defects, and finishing defects
Late submittals, missing samples, and unpaid fees
Owner-caused damage, shipping damage, and installer error
explanation: §5.11 / §5.20 — the three exclusion types are: (a) contract-specified item(s) in variance to AWMAC Standards; (b) project conditions beyond the Manufacturer's control; and (c) Manufacturer compliance issues.

stem: A contract-specified variance of a purely aesthetic nature (no impact on structure or function) may be included in the AWMAC warranty only with whose approval?
options: The AWMAC President and Vice President
The Inspector, the Chapter GIS Representative, AND the GIS Committee Chair
explanation: §5.20.1.a — such aesthetic variances may be included in the AWMAC warranty with approval of the Inspector, the Chapter GIS Representative, AND the GIS Committee Chair; the item stays listed on the Final Inspection Report.

stem: How is the Owner's GIS payment to the Manufacturer treated?
options: It is retained by the Manufacturer as an administration fee
It is held in trust, to be paid to the local Chapter
It is remitted directly to AWMAC National
It is credited against the Manufacturer's annual membership dues
explanation: §5.21.1 — the Owner's GIS payment to the Manufacturer is held in trust to be paid to the local Chapter.

stem: The GIS cost is based on the architectural subcontract plus all change orders. What special rule applies when the woodwork portion is small?
options: If the woodwork is under 50% of the contract, no GIS fee applies
If the woodwork is over 75% of the contract, the fee is halved
If the woodwork portion is less than 75% of the total contract and the project value is over $135,000, the GIS fee is based on the woodwork portion only
The fee is always based on the full contract value regardless of the woodwork portion
explanation: §5.21.2 — if the architectural woodwork portion is less than 75% of the total contract, and the project value is over $135,000, the GIS fee is based on the woodwork portion only.

stem: What is the MSE requirement a Manufacturer Member must satisfy before a Guarantee is issued?
options: At least one owner or manager must have passed the current MSE (that individual, not the company, is the certificate holder)
Every employee must pass the MSE
The company itself is certified once it passes an on-site audit
The Chapter GIS Representative must pass the MSE on the Member's behalf
explanation: §5.18.2 / §5.19.1 — an AWMAC Member must have at least one owner or manager who has passed the current MSE prior to Guarantee issuance; the owner/manager is the MSE certificate holder, not the company.

stem: Approximately how many questions is the Inspector Certification Exam (ICE), and how is its content sourced?
options: ~100 questions; entirely from the GIS Policy and Procedures Manual
~250 questions; split 50/50 between AWMAC Standards and GIS P&P
~150 questions; ~90% from the GIS P&P and ~10% from AWMAC Standards
~250 multiple-choice questions; ~90% from the AWMAC Standards and Errata, ~10% from the GIS Policy and Procedures Manual
explanation: §6.4.2.b — the ICE is approximately 250 multiple-choice questions; ~90% from the current AWMAC Standards and Errata and ~10% from the current GIS Policy and Procedures Manual.

stem: On the ICE, what result makes a prospective Inspector an "Interim Inspector"?
options: A mark of 85% or greater on the first attempt
A mark of 95% or greater (or achieving 95% on a Rewrite after scoring 85% to <95%)
Completion of four mentored projects, regardless of exam mark
explanation: §6.4.3 — 95% or greater makes an Interim Inspector; 85% to <95% requires a Rewrite (Interim if 95% is then achieved); below 85% initially, or below 95% after the Rewrite, is a fail.

stem: Before becoming certified, an Interim Inspector must complete the inspection of how many GIS projects with a mentor Inspector?
explanation: §6.4.5 — an Interim Inspector must complete the inspection of four GIS projects — each including submittals, mock-up and site inspections — done with an existing (mentor) Inspector.

stem: Within how many days must an Inspector complete inspections and reports after receiving documents or notification?
explanation: §6.2.2 — inspections and reports are to be completed within seven days upon receipt of documents (submittal inspection) or notification by the Manufacturer (sample and site inspections).

stem: To whom does the Inspector submit reports, and may the Inspector send them directly to other parties?
options: To the Design Professional directly, with a copy to the Chapter
To the Owner, who distributes them to the other parties
Directly to whichever party requests the report first
To the Chapter office for distribution; under no circumstances directly to other parties
explanation: §6.2.3 / §6.15.13 — all reports are submitted to the Chapter office for distribution; under no circumstances may they be sent directly to other parties by the Inspector.

stem: Where two or more sections of the AWMAC Standards are found to be in conflict, what governs until the conflict is formally clarified?
options: The less restrictive standard requirement prevails until formally clarified in writing by the AWMAC Manual Committee
The Inspector chooses which requirement to apply
explanation: §6.7.4 (and §8.3.3) — where sections conflict, the less restrictive standard requirement prevails until formally clarified in writing by the AWMAC Manual Committee.

stem: How often must existing Inspectors complete an updated ICE?
options: Only when a complaint is filed against them
Within three months of the effective date of a new edition of AWMAC Standards and/or every five years, whichever comes first
Every ten years, matching the Guarantee cycle
explanation: §6.4.16 — all Inspectors complete an updated ICE within three months of the effective date of a new edition of AWMAC Standards and/or every five years, whichever comes first (or at the GIS Committee's discretion).

stem: When stating a matter of opinion (rather than measurable fact) in a report, the Inspector should precede it with which phrase?
explanation: §6.15.8 — fact must be separated from opinion; any statement of opinion should be preceded by the phrase "in my opinion."

stem: At minimum, how often must the Chapter review each Certified GIS Inspector?
explanation: §6.4.15 — the Chapter reviews each Inspector at minimum once every two years, including a walk-through of a current GIS project and completion of the Evaluation Form.

stem: An Inspector must not inspect a project/product where they were previously employed by or contracted to a contractually involved party for a minimum of how long, unless authorized by the GIS Committee?
explanation: §6.1 — an Inspector must be impartial and not inspect any project/product where previously employed by or contracted to any involved party for a minimum of two years, unless authorized by the GIS Committee.

stem: On the GIS Rate Schedule, what is the fee for an architectural woodwork contract valued between $100,001 and $500,000?
explanation: §7.2 — GIS Rates: $0–$100,000 = $1,600 flat fee; $100,001–$500,000 = 1.6%; the rate steps down (1.0%, 0.5%, 0.25%) on higher value bands.

stem: On an inter-Chapter Guarantee, the Manufacturer's Chapter invoices the project Chapter what share of the gross GIS invoice for its liability fund?
explanation: §7.1.7 — for an inter-Chapter Guarantee, the Manufacturer's Chapter invoices the project Chapter five percent (5%) of the gross GIS invoice for their liability fund, plus GST.

stem: How does AWMAC National invoice each Chapter?
options: A flat $5,000 per Chapter per year
12% of the Chapter's gross previous year's GIS revenue, or $280 per Chapter Manufacturer Member, whichever is greater
explanation: §7.5.1 — AWMAC invoices each Chapter the greater of 12% of the Chapter's gross previous-year GIS revenue or $280 per Chapter Manufacturer Member.

stem: When must a non-member pay the GIS inspection cost, and what leverage does the Chapter retain?
options: Net 30 after the final report; no leverage
COD before release of the first report; the Chapter may withhold the Certificate of Guarantee until final payment
Only after the Guarantee is issued; none
In two installments at tender and at completion; none
explanation: §7.1.4–.5 — a non-member is charged COD before release of the first report, and the Chapter has the right to withhold the Certificate of Guarantee until final payment is received.

stem: At what rate is Inspector mileage charged for travel to projects outside the Chapter's hub area?
options: A flat per-trip fee set by AWMAC National
At the Manufacturer's internal fleet rate
explanation: §7.3.1 — mileage is charged at the current CRA (Canada Revenue Agency) suggested rates; travel time is billed hourly at a rate set by each Chapter.

stem: What is the cost of the MSE to a Manufacturer Member?
options: Free — a member benefit, including upgrades and new MSEs
A one-time fee set by the Chapter
Free for the first attempt only, then charged per retake
explanation: §7.8 (GIS P&P) and MSE Policy §5.1–.2 — the MSE, including upgrades or new MSEs, is free for all Manufacturer Member personnel; there is no limit on how many individuals may take it.

stem: What is the stated communication-timeliness standard for GIS interpretations and disputes?
options: Responses within five business days by registered mail
Communication only at scheduled quarterly meetings
Emails answered within 24 hours, documented and electronically filed
explanation: §8.1 — all communication occurs in a timely fashion, e.g. emails answered within 24 hours, documented and electronically filed.

stem: In a dispute between a project and a Manufacturer within the same Chapter that cannot be resolved locally, who makes the final binding decision?
options: The AWMAC National Board of Directors, based on the GIS Committee's recommendation
explanation: §8.5.1 — if unresolved, the matter escalates to the GIS Committee, which recommends to the AWMAC National BOD; the National BOD makes the final, binding decision.

stem: To whom may a party appeal a decision, and what is the status of that body's determination?
options: To the GIS Committee; its decision may be further appealed to the BOD
To the Chapter Board; its decision is advisory only
To the AWMAC Board of Directors; its determination is final and may not be appealed further
To a provincial court; AWMAC decisions are non-binding
explanation: §8.6 — any party may appeal to the AWMAC BOD; if a hearing is granted the BOD makes a final determination that is final and may not be appealed any further.

stem: Complex AWMAC Standards questions go to the Manual Committee Chair, but complex GIS Policy & Procedures questions are forwarded to whom?
explanation: §8.3.2 vs §8.4.2 — complex AWMAC Standards questions go to the AWMAC Manual Committee Chair; complex GIS Policy & Procedures questions go to the AWMAC GIS Chair. (Standards → Manual Committee; P&P → GIS Chair.)

stem: Where are all GIS interpretations and dispute resolutions recorded?
options: In the Inspector's personal project file
In the Certificate of Guarantee register
explanation: §8.2.2 — all interpretations and dispute resolutions are filed in the GIS Control Sheet.

stem: What is the required FIRST step in the mediation procedure for a dispute?
options: Notify the AWMAC National Board of Directors immediately
The parties make every effort possible to resolve the issue on their own
File written documentation with the GIS Committee Chair
Request a hearing before the Adjudicating Committee
explanation: §8.5.1.a — the first step is for the parties involved to make every effort possible to resolve the issue on their own in an efficient manner, before escalating to the Chapter GIS Representative.

stem: What is the stated character of consequences under the Compliance section, at least initially?
options: Purely financial penalties in all cases
Initially informative and educational rather than punitive
explanation: Section 9 Purpose — consequences, depending on the issue, will be initially informative and educational rather than punitive; the GIS Committee reviews all information without bias before deciding an outcome.

stem: What happens when a compliance penalty is not fulfilled within the time allotted?
options: The issue is elevated to the next Tier level
The penalty is waived after a grace period
The matter is referred directly to a provincial court
explanation: §9.2 — penalties not fulfilled in the time allotted will be elevated to the next Tier level.

stem: "Negotiating or requesting the GIS specification be deleted" is classified as which Tier of non-compliance?
explanation: §9.2.4.b / §9.3.4.b — negotiating or requesting the GIS specification be deleted (and not honouring the Certificate of Guarantee conditions) are Tier FOUR issues — the most serious.

stem: Which of the following is a Tier FOUR penalty?
options: A cautionary letter from the Chapter
Immediate suspension of GIS privileges and a request to the Chapter BOD to terminate membership
Two projects inspected over one year at the Member's cost
A three-month suspension plus one to three inspected projects
explanation: §9.5.4 Penalties — Tier FOUR: immediate suspension of GIS privileges, request to the Chapter BOD to terminate membership, with a final appeal available to the AWMAC BOD (whose determination is final).

stem: A Tier ONE compliance issue is administered by whom, and what is the penalty?
options: The Adjudicating Committee; a one-year suspension
The GIS Committee Chair; a monetary fine
The Chapter GIS Representative; a cautionary letter
The AWMAC BOD; termination of membership
explanation: §9.5.1 — Tier ONE is administered by the Chapter GIS Representative; if a Tier ONE violation is found, a caution letter is sent to the Manufacturer and recorded in the National GIS Control Sheet.

stem: Tier TWO (and higher) compliance issues are administered by an Adjudicating Committee made up of whom?
options: Three GIS Representatives, not from the project's or the Member's Chapter
The AWMAC President and two Vice Presidents
The project Inspector and the Chapter GIS Administrator
Any two Manufacturer Members in Good Standing
explanation: §9.4.4 / §9.5.2 — an Adjudicating Committee of three GIS Representatives (not from the project's or the Member's Chapter) is created by the GIS Committee to deal with the non-compliant issues.

stem: AWMAC Standards non-compliant items controlled by the Member must be made good within what guideline time before Tier consequences apply?
explanation: §9.1 — AWMAC Standards non-compliant items controlled by the Manufacturer Member that are not made good within a reasonable time (guideline — two weeks) will be dealt with under the Tier Levels.

stem: Submitting the GIS Inspection Request (GIS IRF) AFTER manufacturing has already started is an example of which Tier of GIS P&P non-compliance?
explanation: §9.3.2.b — a GIS IRF submitted after manufacturing has started is listed as a Tier TWO GIS Policy & Procedures issue.

stem: A Tier THREE penalty includes suspension of GIS privileges for what period?
explanation: §9.5.3 Penalties — Tier THREE: three months to one year suspension of GIS privileges, plus one to three projects inspected over a one-year period at the Member's cost, as determined by the Adjudicating Committee.

stem: What minimum score is required to pass the Manufacturer Standards Exam (MSE)?
explanation: MSE Policy §1.2 — a minimum score of 95% is required to pass the MSE. (Same 95% threshold as the Inspector's ICE.)

stem: May a non-member write the MSE?
options: Yes, if sponsored by a Member
Only if they intend to join AWMAC within 90 days
No — non-members may not write the MSE
explanation: MSE Policy §2.2 — non-members may not write the MSE. Successful completion is tied to maintaining Member Good Standing.

stem: If the only MSE certificate holder leaves a Manufacturer Member company, how long does the company have to have another individual complete the MSE before losing Good Standing?
explanation: MSE Policy §3.2.2–.3 — if the certificate holder was the only certified individual, the Member Chapter notifies the company they have 60 days to have another individual complete the MSE; after 60 days with no holder the Member becomes not in good standing.

stem: Within what period must current Manufacturer Members complete the MSE after a new edition of AWMAC Standards takes effect?
explanation: MSE Policy §4.2 — current Manufacturer Members must complete the MSE within 90 days of the effective date of any new edition of AWMAC Standards, or as required by AWMAC.

stem: What happens to an MSE Certificate holder's status when they move between companies?
options: It remains in effect if they join another Manufacturer Member company, but is marked void if they join a non-member company
It voids in all cases when they change employers
It transfers only with written approval from AWMAC National
It remains in effect for 12 months regardless of the new employer
explanation: MSE Policy §3.1.1–.2 — the certificate follows the individual: it stays in effect at another Member company, but is marked void if the holder joins a non-member company.

stem: If a candidate is unsuccessful on the MSE, what are the retake rules, and when is the certificate issued on success?
options: One retake only; certificate issued within 30 days
No retakes for six months; certificate issued immediately
Three retakes maximum; certificate issued at the next AGM
